coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of near-infrared emission carbon quantum dots as fluorescence aptamer sensor for cortisol sensing and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jing
Liu, Hao
Huang, Yan
Li, Li
Zhu, Xiaoli
Ding, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely applied to sensing and bioimaging. In this paper, near-infrared carbon quantum dots (NIR-CQDs) were prepared through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach using reduced glutathione and formamide as raw materials. Based on NIR-CQDs, aptamer (Apt) and graphene oxide (GO) has been applied to fluorescence sensing cortisol. NIR-CQDs-Apt adsorbed to the surface of GO through π-π stacking and an inner filter effect (IFE) occurred between NIR-CQDs-Apt and GO leading to NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence “off”. The IFE process is disrupted in the presence of cortisol, allowing NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence “on”. This led us to construct a detection method with excellent selectivity over other cortisol sensors. The sensor can detect cortisol from 0.4 to 500 nM and has a detection limit as low as 0.13 nM. Importantly, this sensor can be used to detect intracellular cortisol with excellent biocompatibility and cellular imaging capabilities, which is promising for biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared carbon quantum dots
Aptamer
Fluorescence
Cortisol
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exploring the sensing mechanism of cortisol

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274096 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of NIR-CQDs (Inset: The particle size distribution and HRTEM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of NIR-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c)XPS spectra of NIR-CQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of NIR-CQDs.

(a) Effects of different pH values. (b) Effects of different concentrations of GO. (c) Effects of different volumes of aptamer. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO in the absence and presence of Ct, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO in the presence of different concentrations of Ct. Inset: The Linear plots of (F–F0)/F0 versus the concentrations of Ct. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO in the absence and presence of Ct, correspondingly. (b) Selectivity of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO for detecting Ct.
(a) TEM of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO. (b) The emission of NIR-CQDs and UV–vis of GO. (c) Fluorescence lifetime curve. (d) Fluorescence spectra of different systems.
Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells incubated with NIR-CQDs-Apt and NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO before and after addition of Ct.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence sensing and imaging of cortisol based on the inner filter effect between NIR-CQDs-Apt and graphene oxide.
Detection of Ct in saliva samples.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of near-infrared emission carbon quantum dots as fluorescence aptamer sensor for cortisol sensing and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory Medicine, Shanghai Tenth People's Hospital of Tongji University, Shanghai, 200072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Tenth People's Hospital of Tongji University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory Medicine, Shanghai Tenth People's Hospital of Tongji University, Shanghai 200072, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely applied to sensing and bioimaging. In this paper, near-infrared carbon quantum dots (NIR-CQDs) were prepared through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach using reduced glutathione and formamide as raw materials. Based on NIR-CQDs, aptamer (Apt) and graphene oxide (GO) has been applied to fluorescence sensing cortisol. NIR-CQDs-Apt adsorbed to the surface of GO through π-π stacking and an inner filter effect (IFE) occurred between NIR-CQDs-Apt and GO leading to NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence “off”. The IFE process is disrupted in the presence of cortisol, allowing NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence “on”. This led us to construct a detection method with excellent selectivity over other cortisol sensors. The sensor can detect cortisol from 0.4 to 500 nM and has a detection limit as low as 0.13 nM. Importantly, this sensor can be used to detect intracellular cortisol with excellent biocompatibility and cellular imaging capabilities, which is promising for biosensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cortisol (Ct), produced by the adrenal glands, is a type of steroid hormone [1]. It is in close association with pathological conditions such as anxiety, depression, and cardiovascular disease, also referred to as a stress hormone [2,3]. Therefore, effective and rapid cortisol measuring methods are important to assess for understanding human stress symptoms and health monitoring. Usually, the existing cortisol detection assays are complex, time-consuming, costly, and low-specific, like liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5], lateral flow assays [6], etc. In contrast, the fluorescence method has become the focus of attention in the field of analysis and detection because of the simplicity of its operation, low cost of detection, and high practicality [7,8]. At present, there are few reports on the fluorescence sensor for cortisol, so it is still challenging to develop a fluorescence sensor for sensitive and rapid monitoring of cortisol.

Over the past two decades, various fluorescence sensing materials have been reported, such as fluorescent proteins [9], semiconductor quantum dots [10], fluorescent dye [11], carbon dots [12], and so on. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQD), which are generally smaller than 10 nm, are considered a category of zero-dimensional carbon materials [13]. Owing to their excellent optical characteristics, good biocompatibility and dispersibility, and ease of synthesis and modification [14,15], CQDs hold great promise for applications in chemical sensing, bioimaging, therapeutic diagnostics, and other fields [16–18]. So far, a large number of researchers have explored CQDs, but most CQDs only exhibit strong blue emission under ultraviolet or blue excitation, which is inevitably interfered with by the inherent blue autofluorescence of biological matrix [19,20]. In contrast, fluorescent biosensors constructed from near-infrared carbon quantum dots (NIR-CQDs) can be effective in preventing the interference of autofluorescence, as well as having excellent penetration into cellular tissues [21–23]. Heretofore, there has been some progress in the preparation strategy and primary bioimaging applications of NIR-CQDs, but in reality, only a small amount of work has focused on their biosensing applications. Notably, the development of NIR-CQDs-based biosensors is therefore urgently needed, but the construction of NIR-CQDs biosensors with the expected functionality is also a major challenge.

Ct is generally measured using antibodies as recognition elements to improve affinity and specificity for the target [24,25]. However, antibodies are prone to irreversible denaturation, have relatively strict detection conditions, and are obviously immunogenic. In addition to antibody-based assays, it has been documented that aptamers can also be used to detect cortisol [26,27]. And the aptamers can be used in various buffers as they are comparatively stable at room temperature and are suitable for bioimaging [28–30]. The aptamer fluorescence sensor based on NIR-CQDs has remarkable advantages of great selectivity and high sensitivity. Hence, in this paper, the fluorescence sensor for cortisol detection was constructed for the first time using NIR-CQDs and aptamer.

In the present work, NIR-CQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal approach with reduced glutathione and formamide as raw materials (Scheme 1 ). NIR-CQDs (fluorophores), aptamer (Apt, recognition element), and graphene oxide (GO, quenching agent) consist of the fluorescence “on” aptamer sensor for detection Ct. NIR-CQDs-Apt adsorbs on the GO surface through π-π stacking, and the inner filter effect (IFE) between the NIR-CQDs-Apt and GO leads to fluorescence “off” of NIR-CQDs-Apt. With the specific recognition effect of cortisol and aptamer, NIR-CQDs-Apt detaches from the surface of GO, resulting in the fluorescence “on” of NIR-CQDs-Apt. As expected, the sensor was successfully used in the high-sensitive and high-selective quantitative analysis of cortisol, allowing the determination of cortisol concentrations in human saliva samples within the normal range. Moreover, this sensor has excellent biocompatibility and high-resolution cellular imaging capability in living cells, reflecting its promising application as a powerful clinical tool for detecting cortisol in vivo, leading to early disease diagnosis and intervention.

1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ascorbic acid, NaCl, and KCl were purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide (GO), formamide, reduced glutathione (L-GSH), estradiol, progesterone, testosterone propionate, cholesterol, and estrone were obtained by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cortisol, KH2PO4, and K2HPO4 were obtained by Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The aptamer sequence of cortisol was 5′-GGAATGGATCCACATCCATGGATGGGCAATGCGG GGTGGAGAATGGTTGCCGCACTTCGGCTTCACTGCAGACTTGACGAAGCTT-3′ and was bought by Tsingke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents do not require further preparation before use.

TEM and HRTEM of NIR-CQDs were measured on transmission electron microscopes (JEM-2100F). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded via a UV-3600iPlus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence spectra were collected by using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was recorded via a WSCALAB 250Xi (Thermo). A Nicolet AVATAR 370 spectrometer was employed to obtain FT-IR spectra. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured by an Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorometer.

NIR-CQDs were synthesized based on a previous study [31]. After mixing 510 mg L-GSH and 15 mL formamide homogeneously, transfer to a 50 mL PTFE autoclave. The oven temperature reached 160 °C and was kept for 1 h. The green supernatant was collected via centrifugation (4000 rpm, 20 min) after the reaction was cooled to room temperature. A 0.22 μm filter membrane was applied to filter the supernatant. The purification was then completed through dialysis with a dialysis membrane (MW 3000–5000) for 3 days. NIR-CQDs solution was kept at 4 °C for subsequent research.

The aptamer of cortisol and NIR-CQDs are covalently linked by acylation reaction. 500 μL of EDC (8.7 mM) and 500 μL of NHS (3.2 mM) were mixed homogeneously and added to NIR-CQDs solution (100 μL), then stirred vigorously at room temperature for 1 h. NIR-CQDs-aptamer (NIR-CQDs-Apt) was obtained by stirring the above mixture (110 μL) and aptamer solution (30 μL, 1 μM) for 12 h in the dark.

GO quenching NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence as follows: NIR-CQDs-Apt solution was mixed with the 20 μL GO (500 μg/mL) and incubated for 30 min at room temperature to form NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO. The above solution was diluted to 1.5 mL with pH 7.5 PBS. The fluorescence intensity of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO at 650 nm was recorded under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was represented by F0.

Cortisol recovering NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence as follows: Cortisol in different concentrations was added to the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO solution to recover NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence and diluted to 1.5 mL with pH 7.5 PBS. The fluorescence intensity at 650 nm was measured (excitation wavelength at 420 nm), and the fluorescence intensity was represented by F.

The cytotoxicity of NIR-CQDs was analyzed via the CCK-8 method using HeLa cells. Incubate in an incubator for 24 h (5% CO2, 37 °C) after adding 100 μL of 1 × 105 cells/mL cell suspension to a 96-well plate. NIR-CQDs at concentrations of 0, 10, 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 300 μg/mL were separately added to the 96-well plate, followed by incubation for 24 h. Then the former medium was discarded and replaced with a fresh medium. After adding 10 μL CCK-8 to each well and incubating for 1 h, a microplate reader determined the absorbance at 450 nm. The cell viability without the addition of NIR-CQDs was set at 100%.

HeLa cells were added to the medium containing 5 mL FBS, 500 μL PSA, and 45 mL DMEM and incubated overnight (5% CO2, 37 °C). Then NIR-CQDs-Apt and NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO were added to the confocal dish, followed by incubation for 2 h. HeLa cells were incubated with containing Ct (0, 50, and 500 nM) for 30 min and washed three times using pH 7.4 PBS (0.01 M). Lastly, 1 mL of PBS was added to observe the HeLa cells for imaging under 420 nm excitation.

The NIR-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using glutathione and formamide. It can be observed from Fig. 1 a that, NIR-CQDs are well dispersed with a particle size range of 1.17–3.13 nm. The average size of NIR-CQDs is 2.18 nm. The HRTEM image displayed clear lattice stripes. The lattice stripe spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the crystal plane (100) for graphite, indicating that the NIR-CQDs contain a graphite-like structure [31]. The UV–vis absorption of NIR-CQDs is shown in Fig. S1, showing three distinct absorption regions. The absorption peaks located at 420 nm and 640 nm belong to π→π* and n→π* transitions of the CC, CO, and CS bonds, correspondingly. 265 nm absorption peak belongs to π→π* transition of the aromatic CC [31]. 420 nm and 650 nm are the excitation and emission wavelengths of NIR-CQDs, correspondingly. The excitation wavelength dependence of NIR-CQDs is investigated (Fig. 1b). As the excitation wavelength of NIR CQDs progressively increases (excitation wavelength in the range of 425–445 nm), the emission wavelength is redshifted. This indicates that NIR-CQDs have a certain excitation wavelength dependence.

The microstructure of NIR-CQDs was investigated by XPS and FT-IR. As can be seen in the full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1c), O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p belong to the four peaks of 532 eV, 399 eV, 285 eV, and 168 eV, correspondingly. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S2a), the existence of C–C/CC (284.4 eV), C–S/C–N (285.3 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and CO (288.7 eV) on the surface of NIR-CQDs can be observed [32]. The peaks of the CO (531.7 eV) and C–O–C/C–OH (532.3 eV) bonds are shown in the O 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2b) [33]. The presence of three N types, pyridine N (399.1 eV), pyrrole N (399.9 eV), and N–H (400.8 eV), is indicated in the N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2c) [31]. The S 2p high-resolution spectra (Fig. S2d) show two peaks of 167.1 eV and 169 eV belonging to C-SOx (x = 2 and 4) [14]. The above results indicate that NIR-CQDs are doped with nitrogen and sulfur. FT-IR spectra of NIR-CQDs were subsequently investigated (Fig. 1d), indicating the presence of rich hydrophilic groups on the surface of NIR-CQDs. The stretching vibration of O–H/N–H responds to the strong peak located at 3419 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 1615 cm−1, 1394 cm−1 and 1244 cm−1 belong to CN/CO, C–N, and C–O, correspondingly. The peak located at 1090 cm−1 is attributed to the CS bond.

To achieve the excellent performance of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor, the experimental conditions were optimized, including pH value, GO concentration, and aptamer dose. Since pH could affect the affinity of the aptamer for the target, optimizing pH is critical for NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor to achieve their highest performance. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the influence of different pH values (4.5–9.5) on the detection of cortisol by NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO was studied. After adding cortisol to NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO, the fluorescence recovery reached a maximum at pH 7.5. Therefore, pH 7.5 was used in subsequent research. Then the effect of different concentrations of GO (125–800 μg/mL) on the degree of fluorescence quenching was explored. Fig. 2b demonstrates that the degree of fluorescence quench of NIR-CQDs-Apt gradually increased with increasing GO concentration. And as the GO concentration exceeded 500 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity reached stability, but the high concentration of quenchers would be detrimental to fluorescence recovery. Therefore, the optimum quenching concentration for GO was 500 μg/mL. The recovery of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence is also influenced by the dosage of the aptamer. Therefore, we investigated the effect of different amounts of aptamer (10–100 μL) on fluorescence recovery. As can be seen from Fig. 2c, when the amount of aptamer is too low or too high, the ability of the sensor to capture cortisol is weak, thus hindering the fluorescence recovery. When 30 μL of cortisol aptamer was added, the fluorescence recovery of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO was maximized, so we chose 30 μL as the optimal dosage of cortisol aptamer.

Under optimal experimental conditions, different concentrations of cortisol were added to the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor. It can be seen in Fig. 3 a that the fluorescence intensity of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO progressively increased with increasing the amount of cortisol added. The experiment showed that the fluorescence intensity depended on the concentration of cortisol. (F–F0)/F0 and the concentration of Ct in the range of 0.4–500 nM displayed a good linear relationship. The linear equation was (F–F0)/F0 = 0.03777 + 0.003210[Ct] (R2 = 0.9954), and the limit of detection was measured at 0.13 nM (S/N = 3). Compared with other cortisol sensors, the sensor in this work has a wider linear range and a lower detection limit, which makes it superior to other sensors (as shown in Table S1). Therefore, the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO sensor has great application prospects as a fluorescence sensor for cortisol detection.

To evaluate the selectivity of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor for cortisol, the (F–F0)/F0 of the sensor in the presence of possible interfering substances was studied under optimal experimental conditions. It is known from Fig. 3b, Ct analogs and coexisting substances hardly increased the fluorescence intensity, including estradiol, progesterone, testosterone propionate, cholesterol, estrone, ascorbic acid, NaCl, and KCl, while the fluorescence recovery of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO was obvious after adding cortisol. The high selectivity of the sensor can be analyzed in two aspects: First, the aptamer of cortisol shows a specific binding affinity for the target cortisol. The other is the ability of interfering substances to restore the fluorescence of the sensor. The interfering substances that make the fluorescence recovery weak even if the sensor happens to capture them had basically no influence on the fluorescence intensity, that is, those interfering substances will not be detected [29,34]. Therefore, the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor has good selectivity for cortisol and can specifically identify cortisol from cortisol analogs and coexisting substances, which can be used for the detection of cortisol in a complex matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the sensing mechanism of cortisol
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, NIR-CQDs were attached to aptamer by covalent coupling, and then NIR-CQDs-Apt were adsorbed on the GO surface by π-π stacking. To check the successful adsorption of NIR-CQDs-Apt, TEM images of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO were measured (Fig. 4 a), and it is clearly seen that NIR-CQDs-Apt is dispersed on the GO surface, which directly confirms the successful construction of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO sensor. Fig. 4b shows that the absorption peak of GO and the excitation peak of NIR-CQDs-Apt have a significant overlap. This indicates that GO quenches the fluorescence of NIR-CQDs possibly due to IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [35]. It has been studied that FRET and IFE can be differentiated by measuring the fluorescence lifetime. Fluorescence lifetime decreases owing to energy transfer during FRET, whereas the fluorescence lifetime remains essentially unchanged during IFE [36]. As a consequence, the decay curves of NIR-CQDs-Apt and NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO were subsequently measured. From Fig. 4c, the fluorescence lifetime of NIR-CQDs-Apt is 2.59 ns, and the lifetime of NIR-CQDs-Apt in NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO is 2.62 ns. The lifetime is essentially unchanged indicating that the GO quenched NIR-CQDs-Apt fluorescence is due to the IFE mechanism rather than the FRET mechanism. When Ct was added to NIR-CQDs-Apt and NIR-CQDs/GO, respectively, the fluorescence intensity remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 4d). After the addition of Ct to NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO, the specific combination between Ct and aptamer causes the separation of NIR-CQDs-Apt from the GO surface resulting in the fluorescence recovery of NIR-CQDs-Apt. This indicates that GO and aptamer play a key role in this sensing process. Hence, the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO fluorescence sensor can be used as a novel detection technique for Ct determination.

To validate the real-world application of the sensor, we spiked saliva with different concentrations of cortisol. The saliva samples were diluted three times with pH 7.5 PBS and incubated with the sensor for 30 min for fluorescence detection. Table 1 provides the experimental results, and the recoveries of the assays were 95.0%–106.2% with an RSD of less than 4.7%. This reveals the reliability of the NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO sensor in real sample analysis.

In the aspect of cell imaging, the biocompatibility of sensors is very important for their further biological applications, so the cytotoxicity of NIR-CQDs was estimated by the CCK-8 method. From Fig. S3, the viability of HeLa cells was basically unchanged at concentrations of 0–30 μg/mL of NIR-CQDs. The cell viability was still more than 85%, despite the concentration of NIR-CQDs being as high as 150 μg/mL. As a result, the prepared NIR-CQDs had low toxicity, a good application prospect in the field of fluorescence bioimaging and could be employed further in intracellular imaging.

The feasibility of the fluorescence sensor for intracellular Ct imaging was further monitored with an upright fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 5 , the cells incubated with NIR-CQDs-Apt showed a strong fluorescence signal, and the cells treated with NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO showed a weak fluorescence signal, indicating that both NIR-CQDs-Apt and NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO could enter HeLa cells for cell imaging. To further explore the practicability of the fluorescence sensor for intracellular Ct fluorescence imaging, cells treated with NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO were incubated with Ct for imaging. The fluorescence signal of NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO in cells was progressively stronger with increasing Ct concentration. This indicates that the fluorescence sensor has strong and stable fluorescence efficiency, good biocompatibility, and high sensitivity to Ct detection. It is expected to become an excellent fluorescence sensor in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging.

In summary, we prepared a cortisol fluorescence sensor based on NIR-CQDs and aptamers with high sensitivity and selectivity. The NIR CQDs-Apt/GO cortisol sensor has a very low detection limit of 0.13 nM. The modification of cortisol aptamers on NIR-CQDs improved the selectivity of the sensor. The sensing performance was significantly improved compared to existing cortisol sensors. The sensor also showed satisfactory recovery of cortisol detection in human saliva samples. In addition, the prepared fluorescence sensor has good near-infrared emission and biocompatibility, and NIR-CQDs-Apt/GO exhibits excellent intracellular imaging capability. It could considerably improve the early diagnosis and monitoring of diseases related to cortisol abnormalities. In short, the NIR-CQDs-Apt-based fluorescence sensor in this study has great promise for cortisol detection and can be expanded to detect and analyze other steroid hormones. This simple and effective assay is promising to be an alternative method for the real-time analysis of steroid hormones.

Jing Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hao Liu: Investigation, Review & editing. Yan Huang: Review & editing. Li Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoli Zhu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yaping Ding: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274096).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124637.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A unique NIR dye constructed mitochondrial anchoring fluorescent probe for highly selective selenocysteine detection and imaging in living cells and mice
